perturbation.original_text: The contractual partners undertake to treat confidentially any information they obtain, to use it only for the intended purpose of the particular assignment or project and not to make it di rectly or indirectly accessible to third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The contractual partners should endeavor to treat confidentially any information they obtain, and may use it for purposes they deem related to the assignment or project, and shall make reasonable efforts to avoid direct access by third parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict duty of confidentiality. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'should endeavor,' 'deem related,' and 'reasonable efforts,' weakening the obligation and making it subjective. It is unclear what constitutes 'related' or 'reasonable efforts'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 321a of the Swiss Code of Obligations imposes a strict duty of confidentiality on employees and agents. The modified text, by using terms like 'should endeavor' and 'reasonable efforts,' allows for a lower standard of care and discretion, potentially violating the strict confidentiality requirements under Swiss law for business relationships.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fedlex DE: Vielen Dank, dass Sie sie die Website des Bundesrechts aufgerufen haben; sie ist nur mit einem Javascript-fähigen Browser verfügbar. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. FR: Merci d'avoir rejoint le site du droit fédéral; celui-ci n'est disponible qu'avec un navigateur supportant javascript. Afin de profiter d'une expérience maximale sur ce site, nous vous conseillons d'utiliser un navigateur récent. IT: Grazie per aver scelto il sito web del diritto federale; questo è disponibile soltanto con un browser che supporta JavaScript. Per navigare in modo ottimale in questo sito, vi consigliamo di utilizzare un browser più recente. RM: Grazia fitg che Vus visitais la pagina d’internet dal dretg federal; ella è disponibla mo cun in navigatur che sustegna javascript. Per pudair far diever da questa pagina d’internet en moda optimala As recumandain nus d’utilisar in navigatur actual. EN: Thank you for visiting the federal law website; it is only fully accessible if you are using a JavaScript-capable browser. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser.
perturbation.justification: The original text enforces a strict confidentiality obligation, while the changed text weakens this obligation by using terms like 'should endeavor,' 'deem related,' and 'reasonable efforts.' The LLM's law explanation indicates that Art. 321a of the Swiss Code of Obligations requires a strict duty of confidentiality. The modification allows for a lower standard of care, contradicting this strict confidentiality requirement. While there are no valid law snippets, the Law Explanation coupled with the significant weakening of the original obligation leads to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: They shall ensure that access to this information, including information in electronic form in particular, is adequately protected.
perturbation.changed_text: They should attempt to ensure that access to this information is generally protected, with consideration given to the practicality of securing electronic information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires ensuring adequate protection. The modified text uses 'should attempt,' 'generally protected,' and 'consideration given to the practicality,' weakening the obligation and introducing subjectivity in determining what constitutes adequate protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP), Art. 7
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 7 of the Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP) requires data controllers to ensure appropriate security measures to protect personal data. The modified text's use of 'should attempt' and consideration of 'practicality' weakens this obligation, potentially leading to inadequate data protection measures, violating the FADP.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates ensuring adequate protection, creating a clear obligation. The changed text uses 'should attempt,' 'generally protected,' and 'consideration given to the practicality,' which weakens the obligation significantly. This shift introduces subjectivity and reduces the enforceability of the protection measures. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this weakening and potential violation of data protection requirements. While the provided law snippets do not contain specific legal text, the Law Explanation suggests that this change could lead to inadequate data protection measures violating the Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP), thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon completion of an assignment or project, the contractual partners undertake to destroy immediately any information or objects given to them that they or any sub -contractors may have, unless such information and property are required for any outstanding or future follow -up activities under the mutual business partnership.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon perceived completion of an assignment or project, the contractual partners will make reasonable efforts to dispose of information or objects they possess, provided they are not subjectively deemed necessary for potential future activities.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires immediate destruction upon completion. The modified text introduces ambiguity with 'perceived completion,' 'reasonable efforts to dispose of,' and 'subjectively deemed necessary,' making the obligation discretionary and open to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 400 of the Swiss Code of Obligations requires agents to return anything received on behalf of the principal. This encompasses physical documents and information. By changing the obligation to 'reasonable efforts to dispose of', the altered text contradicts this obligation of returning everything.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires immediate destruction of information upon completion, while the changed text introduces 'perceived completion,' 'reasonable efforts to dispose of,' and 'subjectively deemed necessary,' making the obligation discretionary. The Law Explanation references Art. 400 of the Swiss Code of Obligations, stating agents must return everything received. While the scraped snippets do not directly support this, the explanation, combined with the altered text's discretionary language ('reasonable efforts,' 'subjectively deemed necessary'), creates a situation where the obligation to return everything is weakened. This discretionary approach contradicts the underlying principle of the agent's duty as articulated in the law explanation, thus there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Business Partner and any sub -contractors may only mention to third parties the Bomatec company name (including illustrated material in connection with Bomatec ) as well as activities Non-Disclosure Agreement_ALL_englisch.docx Date issued: 2016- 10-03 Page 3 of 4 carried out during the collaboration with Bomatec after the prior written approval of Bomatec ’s Executive Board.
perturbation.changed_text: The Business Partner and any sub-contractors should generally refrain from mentioning to third parties the Bomatec company name or activities carried out during the collaboration without considering obtaining informal consent from Bomatec.
perturbation.explanation: The original text strictly prohibits mentioning Bomatec without written approval. The modified text uses 'should generally refrain,' 'without considering obtaining informal consent,' weakening the prohibition and introducing ambiguity about what constitutes 'informal consent,' and makes it subjective by removing the explicit 'written approval'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 3 of the Swiss Unfair Competition Act (UWG) prohibits actions that unfairly exploit or impair the reputation or competitive position of another. By implying that mentions of Bomatec are permissible with just consideration, the changed text potentially allows for actions that damage Bomatec's reputation, contradicting the law's intent to prevent unfair competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 3 UWG - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Gesetz gegen den unlauteren Wettbewerb (UWG) § 3 Verbot unlauterer geschäftlicher Handlungen (1) Unlautere geschäftliche Handlungen sind unzulässig. (2) Geschäftliche Handlungen, die sich an Verbraucher richten oder diese erreichen, sind unlauter, wenn sie nicht der unternehmerischen Sorgfalt entsprechen und dazu geeignet sind, das wirtschaftliche Verhalten des Verbrauchers wesentlich zu beeinflussen. (3) Die im Anhang dieses Gesetzes aufgeführten geschäftlichen Handlungen gegenüber Verbrauchern sind stets unzulässig. (4) Bei der Beurteilung von geschäftlichen Handlungen gegenüber Verbrauchern ist auf den durchschnittlichen Verbraucher oder, wenn sich die geschäftliche Handlung an eine bestimmte Gruppe von Verbrauchern wendet, auf ein durchschnittliches Mitglied dieser Gruppe abzustellen. Geschäftliche Handlungen, die für den Unternehmer vorhersehbar das wirtschaftliche Verhalten nur einer eindeutig identifizierbaren Gruppe von Verbrauchern wesentlich beeinflussen, die auf Grund von geistigen oder körperlichen Beeinträchtigungen, Alter oder Leichtgläubigkeit im Hinblick auf diese geschäftlichen Handlungen oder die diesen zugrunde liegenden Waren oder Dienstleistungen besonders schutzbedürftig sind, sind aus der Sicht eines durchschnittlichen Mitglieds dieser Gruppe zu beurteilen. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular
perturbation.justification: The original contract strictly requires prior written approval from Bomatec's Executive Board before mentioning the company. The changed text weakens this by using "should generally refrain" and suggesting "informal consent" is sufficient, which introduces ambiguity. The Law Explanation suggests that this change could potentially allow actions that damage Bomatec's reputation, violating Art. 3 of the Swiss Unfair Competition Act (UWG). Scraped Law Snippet 1, "§ 3 Verbot unlauterer geschäftlicher Handlungen (1) Unlautere geschäftliche Handlungen sind unzulässig," supports the idea that unfair exploitation of reputation is prohibited, which the ambiguous language in the changed text could facilitate.
